title: Fathers Of The Bride

jokes.text: Tony describes his mundane daily routine, then says he was 'thinking I had it made' when Bobby calls it depressing
jokes.setup: Tony describes a monotonous daily routine that Bobby finds depressing
jokes.punchline: Tony reveals he thinks this routine means he 'had it made'
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that reveals Tony's simple, optimistic character. The setup makes the audience expect agreement about depression, but Tony's genuine satisfaction with routine is both surprising and endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I wake up, I go to work, I come home, I eat, I sleep. Same thing every day.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Tony, that's depressing.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Depressing? Are you kidding? I had it made!

jokes.text: Louie asks Alex a normal human question, then Alex asks about Louie's cockroach problem as his reciprocal 'normal' question
jokes.setup: Alex is surprised Louie asked a normal human question and says it's a two-way street
jokes.punchline: Alex's idea of a normal human question is asking about cockroach problems
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing both Alex's sardonic nature and the dysfunctional workplace dynamics. The cockroach question perfectly captures what passes for normal conversation in this environment.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: It's a two-way street, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, you ask me a normal human question for once.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Like what?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Like, 'Hey Alex, how do you get rid of cockroaches?'

jokes.text: A cockroach appears right after they discuss Louie's cockroach problem
jokes.setup: They just finished discussing Louie's cockroach problem
jokes.punchline: A cockroach immediately appears as if on cue
jokes.explanation: Simple visual callback that works but isn't particularly clever. The timing is good but it's fairly predictable physical comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I haven't seen a cockroach in here in weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's great, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, I think I finally got rid of them all.
A cockroach immediately appears.

jokes.text: Louie tells someone 'Looked like you, Iggy' about the cockroach
jokes.setup: A cockroach has just appeared
jokes.punchline: Louie cruelly compares it to Iggy's appearance
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie cruelty that fits his character perfectly. Simple insult but economical and character-appropriate. Not particularly clever but serves the character dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Hey, look at that cockroach. You know, Iggy, it looks just like you.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: What? That's not funny, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm serious. Same ugly mug, same crooked legs, same pathetic expression.

jokes.text: Banta claims 'This is America' when Louie kicks him out, Louie responds 'No, it isn't! This garage is Louie Land. You want to go to America? Go outside.'
jokes.setup: Banta invokes his American rights to stay and listen
jokes.punchline: Louie declares the garage is 'Louie Land' and America is outside
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist moment that perfectly captures Louie's megalomania and twisted logic. 'Louie Land' is instantly quotable and the escalation from democracy to dictatorship is both funny and character-revealing.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: This is Louie Land. America is outside.

jokes.text: Someone describes Alex's eyes as 'bulging like a frog's in heat'
jokes.setup: Alex is anxiously waiting to hear what Louie knows
jokes.punchline: His anticipation is described with the oddly specific simile of a frog in heat
jokes.explanation: Vivid, specific imagery that's both unexpected and amusing. The specificity of 'frog in heat' elevates what could have been a generic description.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: So what is it, Louie? What do you know?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're jumping around like a frog in heat.

jokes.text: Louie tells someone to stick around because Alex will need their 'ample bosom to cry on'
jokes.setup: Louie is about to deliver bad news to Alex
jokes.punchline: He crudely references someone's chest while setting up comfort
jokes.explanation: Typical Louie inappropriateness that's crude but fits his character. The formal phrasing 'ample bosom' makes it slightly funnier than a cruder reference would be.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alex, I got some bad news for you. Why don't you sit down? Here, put your head on my chest.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, what's wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Your mother called. She's not coming to visit.

jokes.text: Louie delivers the devastating news that Alex's daughter is getting married and didn't invite 'the man whose glands gave her birth'
jokes.setup: Alex is anxiously waiting for bad news about his daughter
jokes.punchline: The absurd clinical phrase 'whose glands gave her birth' in an emotional moment
jokes.explanation: Louie's characteristically crude yet clinical way of describing fatherhood adds dark humor to a genuinely painful moment. The biological reductionism is both funny and cruel.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What do you mean she's in the hospital? What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Relax, Alex. The doctor said it's nothing serious. He said her glands gave her birth.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Her glands gave her birth?

jokes.text: Alex says about raising his daughter: 'You give them two of the best years of your life'
jokes.setup: Alex is lamenting how children forget their parents after all they've done
jokes.punchline: He reveals he only gave two years, inadvertently explaining the problem
jokes.explanation: Brilliant self-undermining joke where Alex accidentally reveals why his relationship with his daughter failed. The economy is perfect - shows his self-delusion and explains the situation in one line.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know, it's amazing. You give your whole life to your kids, and they forget about you.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, I gave two years.

jokes.text: Louie's mother's wisdom: 'You're better off raising tomatoes. You can eat 'em.'
jokes.setup: Discussion about ungrateful children who forget their parents
jokes.punchline: Practical advice that tomatoes are better because they're edible
jokes.explanation: Dark but practical wisdom that's both cynical and oddly logical. The bluntness of 'you can eat 'em' is perfectly economical and reveals the family's pragmatic approach to relationships.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what? Forget about kids. They grow up, they forget about you. You want my advice? Grow tomatoes instead.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: At least when a tomato grows up, you can eat it.

jokes.text: Louie trying to comfort Alex: 'Come on, it's not like it's money. Come on.'
jokes.setup: Louie attempting to console Alex about missing his daughter's wedding
jokes.punchline: Louie's idea of comfort is that it's not a financial loss
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment - his attempt at empathy reveals his complete lack of emotional intelligence.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Look at it this way, Alex. You didn't have to pay for the wedding.

jokes.text: Alex: 'Rio De Janeiro to here... Do you know how far that is? Six thousand... eight hundred and forty-two miles.'
jokes.setup: Alex trying to rationalize the mail delay
jokes.punchline: Impossibly specific distance knowledge
jokes.explanation: Shows Alex's cabbie knowledge and desperate rationalization, though somewhat predictable for the character type.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: The mail is delayed because the postal service has to deliver to every address in the country. Do you know how far that is?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: It's 3.6 million miles. Give or take.

jokes.text: 'The Brazilian mail system is government-run with an annual budget of 27,485,000 pesetas.'
jokes.setup: Character providing specific information to support Alex's theory
jokes.punchline: Impossibly detailed and probably made-up statistics
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor with the specificity making it funnier, though we don't know who's saying it.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown character
dialogue.line: I've got statistics that prove it. Seventy-three percent of all cabdrivers in this city have been hit by at least one passenger. And of those, eighty-nine percent were hit on a Tuesday. Furthermore, studies show that redheads are forty-two percent more likely to be involved in a cabfare dispute than any other hair color.

jokes.text: Character admits: 'I wasn't even sure if pesetas was the right money'
jokes.setup: Just gave very specific Brazilian budget information
jokes.punchline: Reveals complete ignorance about the currency while maintaining the false authority
jokes.explanation: Perfect follow-up that deflates the false authority while maintaining the character's obliviousness. Good callback structure that pays off the previous absurd specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown
dialogue.line: Just gave very specific Brazilian budget information

jokes.text: Alex says about the cost of calling Brazil: 'You just went to the well once too often'
jokes.setup: Someone suggests expensive statistics to justify Alex not calling
jokes.punchline: Alex recognizes the pattern of made-up information
jokes.explanation: Alex calling out the running gag of fake statistics shows his awareness and adds meta-humor. Good character moment showing Alex isn't completely gullible.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... I know what you're doing. You're making up statistics to justify why I shouldn't call her.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's the same thing my mother does when she doesn't want me to visit!

jokes.text: Tony misunderstands that Alex gave his daughter an estate in Majorca as a wedding gift
jokes.setup: Reading about the bride's father giving the couple an estate
jokes.punchline: Tony assumes it was Alex, not realizing there's a stepfather
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tony's innocent confusion about the family situation. The misunderstanding highlights Alex's diminished role as father.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Wait, her father gave them an estate? That's wonderful!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, actually it was her stepfather.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Her stepfather? I thought you said her father gave it to them.

jokes.text: Alex on phone: 'I'm calling from New York City on a middle-class income'
jokes.setup: Alex is trying to get through to his daughter quickly
jokes.punchline: He explains his urgency with economic self-awareness
jokes.explanation: Clever way of explaining urgency that reveals class consciousness and economic reality. The phrasing is both specific and universally relatable.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I need to get through to my daughter right away. It's important.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Every minute I'm not working, I'm losing money. And every minute I'm working, I'm losing my daughter.

jokes.text: Louie coaching Alex: 'Reel her in, Rieger. She's bitten the hook. Reel her in!'
jokes.setup: Elaine has offered to accompany Alex to the wedding
jokes.punchline: Louie treats romantic pursuit like fishing with predatory enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie moment showing his crude approach to relationships. The fishing metaphor fits his predatory character and the repetition adds emphasis.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're going to a wedding with her? That's good, that's good. You soften 'em up at the wedding, get 'em emotional with all that love stuff, then you move in for the kill.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, that's not how it works.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Sure it is. Women are like fish. You gotta know when to set the hook.

jokes.text: Doorman checking list: 'Because it's on the list that tells me you're not on the list.'
jokes.setup: Alex asking how he knows so fast that Alex isn't invited
jokes.punchline: There's a separate list specifically for uninvited people
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic - the idea of maintaining a 'not invited' list is perfectly bureaucratic and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Doorman
dialogue.line: There's a separate list specifically for uninvited people

jokes.text: Elaine asking for a light: 'You don't have a cigarette.' 'We don't need a cigarette.'
jokes.setup: Elaine asking doorman for a light
jokes.punchline: Doesn't have cigarette but still wants light anyway
jokes.explanation: Mildly absurd flirtation tactic, setup for Elaine's scheme to distract the doorman.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Do you have a light?

dialogue.speaker: Doorman
dialogue.line: I don't have a cigarette, but I'll give you a light anyway.

jokes.text: Character explains their instant chemistry with a stranger as something their 'older brother' (Alex) doesn't like
jokes.setup: Elaine is flirting with wedding security to get them in
jokes.punchline: She frames Alex as her disapproving older brother rather than date
jokes.explanation: Clever improvisation that serves the plot while creating character comedy. The 'older brother' cover story is both practical and amusing.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: This is my older brother, Alex. He's very disapproving.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'm not her brother.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He's in denial.

jokes.text: Alex says his ex-wife walked out 'before they accepted my application at the cab academy'
jokes.setup: Alex explains his wife left him for being a failure
jokes.punchline: He suggests becoming a cab driver was a major achievement that came too late
jokes.explanation: Brilliant self-deprecating humor that shows Alex's awareness of his modest circumstances while maintaining dignity. The idea of 'cab academy' as an achievement is perfectly crafted irony.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, my wife left me because she said I was a failure. So I became a cab driver.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I guess that was too little, too late.

jokes.text: Alex seeing Phyllis: 'It looks like she put on a couple of extra pounds here and there... and everywhere.'
jokes.setup: Alex diplomatically noting Phyllis gained some weight
jokes.punchline: Escalates from 'here and there' to 'everywhere'
jokes.explanation: Good escalating structure but somewhat mean-spirited setup for later conflict.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Phyllis, you've put on a little weight here and there.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: Here and there? Alex, it's everywhere!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but I was trying to be diplomatic.

jokes.text: Phyllis is introduced pulling up Carlo's socks, saying 'I was just pulling up his socks, dear' when caught
jokes.setup: Cathy asks what her mother is doing down by Carlo's feet
jokes.punchline: Phyllis's overly attentive, slightly inappropriate sock adjustment
jokes.explanation: Simple physical comedy that establishes Phyllis's fawning behavior toward her new wealthy husband. The awkwardness is the point but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Cathy
dialogue.line: What are you doing down there by Carlo's feet?

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: Just adjusting his socks, dear. They were bunched up. A man's comfort is important.

jokes.text: Phyllis: 'I hear they have those electronic meters now.'
jokes.setup: Phyllis trying to make small talk about Alex's work
jokes.punchline: Condescending reference to basic taxi technology as if it's impressive
jokes.explanation: Perfect passive-aggressive dig disguised as interested conversation.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: So Alex, tell me about your work. Do you use those fancy meters in your cab?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yes, Phyllis, we have taximeters. They measure distance and time.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: How absolutely fascinating! And does it have, you know, numbers on it?

jokes.text: Phyllis asks about female cab drivers: 'Where else would all the little cab drivers come from?'
jokes.setup: Elaine explains she's a cab driver
jokes.punchline: Phyllis's condescending biological reduction of the profession
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Phyllis's snobbishness and social awkwardness. The breeding joke is both offensive and revealing of her class attitudes.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: A cab driver? Well, I suppose that's very... practical. Though I must say, it's hardly the sort of thing one aspires to. I mean, sitting in a car all day, dealing with common people, no real intellectual stimulation. It's really just operating a vehicle, isn't it? Anyone with a driver's license could do it.

jokes.text: Alex's Freudian slip: 'you haven't lost a daughter, you've gained a ton' instead of 'gained a son'
jokes.setup: Alex tries to give a traditional wedding congratulation
jokes.punchline: His weight comment comes out as 'gained a ton' instead of 'son'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Freudian slip that perfectly captures Alex's subconscious focus on Phyllis's weight gain. The wordplay is economical and the cringe factor is earned through character motivation.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'd like to propose a toast to the happy couple. May you have all the happiness you deserve. And I'd like to say, in all my years, I've never seen a bride look more beautiful, and groom who's gained a ton.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: A ton?

jokes.text: At the wedding, a man warns his dance partner about Alex: 'Don't let him' and 'He looks like a bad dancer'
jokes.setup: Alex wants to cut in on Phyllis's dance
jokes.punchline: Her partner's immediate assessment that Alex looks incompetent
jokes.explanation: Simple observational humor that reinforces Alex's outsider status at the elegant wedding. Not particularly clever but serves character dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown man
dialogue.line: No way! You look like you don't know what you're doing!

jokes.text: Phyllis accuses Alex: 'You've been sneaking out and taking dancing lessons, haven't you?'
jokes.setup: The man just warned she shouldn't dance with Alex because he's a bad dancer
jokes.punchline: Phyllis sarcastically suggests he's been secretly improving
jokes.explanation: Good callback that plays off the previous dancing comment while showing Phyllis's sarcastic nature and their familiar banter.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: Oh, maybe he's been taking lessons in secret.

jokes.text: Rapid-fire exchange: 'I was afraid I may have hurt your feelings.' 'You wish you could hurt my feelings.' 'Look, if I hurt your feelings...' 'You didn't hurt my feelings.' etc.
jokes.setup: Alex trying to apologize
jokes.punchline: Circular argument that goes nowhere but reveals their dynamic
jokes.explanation: Excellent recreation of married couple argument pattern - escalating contradictions that perfectly capture their relationship.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Phyllis, I'm sorry about what I said.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: Well, you should be!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I know, that's why I'm apologizing.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: An apology doesn't change what you said.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: No, but it shows I regret saying it.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: You should regret it. You hurt my feelings.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Which is exactly why I apologized.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: Apologizing doesn't un-hurt my feelings!

jokes.text: Phyllis threatens: 'You get your hands off me or I'll charge you for half this wedding'
jokes.setup: Alex is trying to get her to talk privately
jokes.punchline: She threatens to make him pay for the expensive wedding he wasn't invited to
jokes.explanation: Clever threat that uses the wedding's expense as leverage while highlighting Alex's exclusion from financial responsibility. Sharp and character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Phyllis, can we talk privately for a moment?

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: You're going to pay for that expensive wedding you weren't invited to.

jokes.text: Phyllis criticizes Alex's apology: 'You took me all the way in here to do what you had to do as badly as you just did it?'
jokes.setup: Alex gives a halfhearted apology for his weight comments
jokes.punchline: Phyllis calls out the inadequacy of his effort with sharp precision
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Phyllis's ability to cut to the heart of Alex's emotional shortcomings. The phrasing captures her disappointment precisely.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'm sorry for what I said about your weight. I was out of line.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: Out of line? Alex, you weren't out of line — you were barely in the same zip code as a compliment.

jokes.text: Phyllis reveals she gained 27 pounds and had to let out her month-old dress three times
jokes.setup: Phyllis explains her self-consciousness about her appearance
jokes.punchline: The specific details of rapid weight gain and dress alterations
jokes.explanation: Honest character moment that explains her defensiveness while providing specific, relatable details about stress eating and appearance anxiety.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: I've gained weight so fast, I had to let out all my dresses. My seamstress said, 'Phyllis, at this rate, I'll have to let out your dresses faster than you can eat.' I said, 'That's impossible!'

jokes.text: Alex mimics Phyllis and she snaps: 'I hate being mimicked. I hate it!'
jokes.setup: Alex reverts to old habits of mocking Phyllis
jokes.punchline: Her explosive reaction shows this was a persistent marital issue
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing old patterns reasserting themselves. Reveals relationship dynamics and shows why they divorced.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Oh Phyllis, still wearing that same outfit from 1987? I didn't know they still made polyester!

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: That's it! I am SICK of this! You think you're so funny, but you've been doing this our entire marriage! Mocking me, belittling me, making me feel small!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Come on, I was just joking around...

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: No! This stops NOW! Either you respect me or we're done!

jokes.text: Alex and Phyllis have a moment where he accurately guesses what she was thinking, leading to 'Lucky guess'
jokes.setup: They're having an intimate conversation about their past relationship
jokes.punchline: Their mental/physical connection is still strong despite years apart
jokes.explanation: Sweet moment showing their continued connection. The 'lucky guess' deflects the intimacy while acknowledging it.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know, Phyllis, I've thought about you over the years.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: I've thought about you too.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I can tell. I can always read you.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: And I can still feel what you're thinking right now.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: See? We haven't lost it.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: No, we haven't. We never will.

jokes.text: Phyllis's vulnerable admission: 'Or find my buttons anymore'
jokes.setup: Discussion about whether they can still 'push each other's buttons'
jokes.punchline: Phyllis fears aging has made her less attractive/responsive
jokes.explanation: Poignant double entendre about both emotional and physical responsiveness. The metaphor works on multiple levels and shows vulnerable character development.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: I'm afraid that after all these years, I've lost my ability to push your buttons.

dialogue.speaker: Danny
dialogue.line: Are you kidding? You still push my buttons.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: Really? Even now?

dialogue.speaker: Danny
dialogue.line: Especially now. Age has made you even more attractive.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: You're just saying that.

dialogue.speaker: Danny
dialogue.line: No, I mean it. You know what? You push all my buttons. Old buttons, new buttons, buttons I didn't even know I had.

jokes.text: Alex's gallant response: 'Well, listen, if they ever get up a search party...'
jokes.setup: Phyllis expresses insecurity about her attractiveness
jokes.punchline: Alex offers to volunteer for the 'search' for her buttons
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback that turns Phyllis's insecurity into romantic reassurance. The military/rescue metaphor is both funny and touching, showing Alex at his most charming.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: I've lost some buttons off my blouse and I'm worried no one will notice me without them.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Don't worry, Phyllis. I'll volunteer to search for those buttons.

jokes.text: Alex's crude observation: 'Five minutes later, you would've had a better gift'
jokes.setup: Cathy says seeing her parents together is the best wedding gift
jokes.punchline: Alex implies they were about to have sex
jokes.explanation: Typical Alex inappropriate comment that deflects emotional intimacy with crude humor. In character but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Cathy
dialogue.line: Seeing my parents together is the best wedding gift I could ask for.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, don't look now, but I think they're about to give each other a really special gift.

jokes.text: Alex comments on the final hug: 'Whoa, we're just being crazy, you know?'
jokes.setup: Alex and Phyllis share an emotional reconciliation moment
jokes.punchline: Alex immediately deflects the intensity by calling it crazy
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Alex's inability to fully embrace emotional moments. The deflection is true to his character's emotional limitations.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: This is crazy.

tmdb_overview: Alex learns through a wedding notice in the newspaper that his daughter is getting married in New York. Alex is very upset that he hasn't been invited and calls his ex-wife, whose secretary explains that he was specifically not invited. Alex crashes the reception anyway, bringing Elaine with him. Cathy, his daughter, is thrilled to see him and asks why he didn't respond to her invitation. Alex gets angry again and tells off his ex-wife, insulting her weight in the process. Alex eventually feels guilty that he insulted his ex-wife and he pulls her aside and apologizes. They then start to reminisce and soon find that they are still somewhat attracted to each other. When they are about to kiss, Cathy finds them and lets them know how happy she is to see them together and asks them to hug. They do, and everyone is on good terms at the end.
insight: Wedding prep chaos yields steady laughs but lacks standout moments at 1.72 jokes-per-minute.
